Jared Smith | False Alerts in Cook County’s Electronic Monitoring System¶

June 2025¶

Research Question:¶

My project aims to study potential associations between electronic monitoring false alerts and Chicago's built environment.

Electronic monitoring is a form of remote surveillance law enforcement uses as an alternative to incarceration: a person on house arrest may be tracked via a GPS ankle monitor, for example. However, GPS has limitations that can affect the accuracy of its monitoring: GPS signals are compromised by interference in densely built-up urban areas. This can lead to 'false alerts', when a GPS error makes it seem like a person is breaking the terms of their home arrest, even though no violation occurred. There are reports that false alerts have caused rule-following supervisees to be harassed by law enforcement.

I will measure the rate of false alerts by zip code. Is there a relationship between the rate of false alerts and zip codes with high building heights? Is the relationship between the rate of false alerts and zip codes with a high building density?

Through my code, I will run a correlation analysis between the rate of false alerts and average building height and building density. I will also map these variables to analayze a potential spatial realtionship.

Data Overview:¶

I am using two main datasets for this project. My Electronic Monitoring False Alert data comes from the Cook County Sheriff's Office, and my Chicago building structure data comes from the FEMA USA Struture Data Set. I will also be using a shapefile of Chicago's Zip codes in order to map the data.

FEMA USA Structure Data Set: https://gis-fema.hub.arcgis.com/pages/usa-structures¶

  • FEMA's USA Structures dataset is a comprehensive inventory of all structures larger than 450 square feet. From this data, I created a dataset that only includes the structures in Chicago.
  • Each instance in this data set is a structure: there are 557,540 structures in Chicago.
  • The main fields of intrest are "HEIGHT" and "SQ METERS". "HEIGHT" is the individual height for each building, and "SQ METERS" is the individual building size. All measurements are in meters.
  • I am defining building density as the sum of the total building size per zip code divided by the area of the zip code.
  • According to the FEMA ArcGIS page, the USA Structures data was collected in 2021 and last updated on April 23, 2025.

False Alert by Zip Code:¶

  • The False Alerts by Zip Code data come directly from the Cook County Sheriff's Office.
  • Each instance in the False Alert dataset is a Zip Code: 164 instances.
  • The False Alert data set only contains three columns: 'Zip' identifies the Zip Code, 'Total Alerts' is a count of all alerts that took place in the Zip Code, and 'False Alerts' is the number of alerts in a zip code determined to be false.
  • The False Alerts data collected the total number of alerts and false alerts between January 2022 and November 2023.

Workflow Overview¶

  • My primary goal is to merge the Chicago Structures, False Alert by Zip Code, and Zip Code Shapefiles into a single dataset organized by zip code. The False Alert by Zip Code and Zip Code Shapefiles are already organized by zip code, so the majority of my work is focused on organizing the Chicago Structures dataset.
  • 10,672 structures did not have an assigned zip code; they contained “null” values in the zip code column. However, since the structural data does contain the latitude and longitude of these structures, I was able to join these structures to the zip code shapefile table spatially.
  • After the null zip codes were updated, I was able to organize the structural data by zip code.
  • I was able to find the area of the Zip Codes by using the Zip Codes shapefile, which allowed me to find the average building density per square meter for each zip code. I was then able to combine the structural data and false alert data to the zip code shapefile by using the zip code as a common key.
  • I then was able to run a correlation analysis and map the geospatial data.

Code: Import Data¶

Import Packages¶

In [1]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import seaborn as sns
import scipy.stats
from scipy.stats import spearmanr

Reading Data Files¶

In [2]:
#Get False Alert by Data Object
faz = gpd.read_file('data/falseAlerts_byZip_OG.xlsx')
In [3]:
#Get Chicago Building Structure Data Object
cbs = gpd.read_file('data/Chicago_structures_raw.xlsx')
In [4]:
#Zip code Shapefiles
path = 'https://data.cityofchicago.org/resource/unjd-c2ca.geojson'
zcodes = gpd.read_file(path)

Code: Data Wrangling¶

Clean Up False Alert by Zip Code (FAZ) Data¶

In [5]:
faz.columns
Out[5]:
Index(['OBJECTID', 'ZIP_CODE', 'PO_NAME', 'STATE', 'POPULATION', 'POP_SQMI',
       'SQMI', 'ZIP_int', 'Zip', 'Total_Alerts', 'False_Alerts',
       'Shape_Length', 'Shape_Area'],
      dtype='object')
In [6]:
#drop uneccesary columns
faz = faz.drop(columns = ['PO_NAME', 'STATE', 'POPULATION', 'POP_SQMI', 'SQMI', 'ZIP_int', 'Zip'])
In [7]:
# drop row with NuLL Zip Codes
faz = faz.drop([56])

Replace [NULL] Zip Code Values with the structure's Zip Code¶

Change Data Type¶

In [13]:
cbs.dtypes
Out[13]:
OBJECTID          int32
BUILD_ID          int32
PROP_ZIP         object
HEIGHT          float64
SQMETERS        float64
SQFEET          float64
LONGITUDE       float64
LATITUDE        float64
Shape_Length    float64
Shape_Area      float64
dtype: object
In [14]:
cbs['PROP_ZIP'] = cbs['PROP_ZIP'].astype('float64')

Export rows with null zip codes to its own table¶

In [15]:
cond = cbs['PROP_ZIP'].isnull()
cbs_null_faz = cbs[cond]
cbs_null_faz = gpd.GeoDataFrame(cbs_null_faz)

Convert polygon to central point using Points From XY¶

In [17]:
cbs_null_faz['geometry']=gpd.points_from_xy(cbs_null_faz['LONGITUDE'], cbs_null_faz['LATITUDE'])

C:\Users\jared\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13436\295435264.py:1: FutureWarning: You are adding a column named 'geometry' to a GeoDataFrame constructed without an active geometry column. Currently, this automatically sets the active geometry column to 'geometry' but in the future that will no longer happen. Instead, either provide geometry to the GeoDataFrame constructor (GeoDataFrame(... geometry=GeoSeries()) or use `set_geometry('geometry')` to explicitly set the active geometry column.
  cbs_null_faz['geometry']=gpd.points_from_xy(cbs_null_faz['LONGITUDE'], cbs_null_faz['LATITUDE'])

Spatially join zip codes shapefiles (polygon) to cbs_null_faz (point)¶

zcodes.crs

- Prime Meridian: Greenwich

Clean Up Merged Data¶

In [26]:
null_zcodes.columns
Out[26]:
Index(['OBJECTID', 'BUILD_ID', 'PROP_ZIP', 'HEIGHT', 'SQMETERS', 'SQFEET',
       'LONGITUDE', 'LATITUDE', 'Shape_Length', 'Shape_Area', 'geometry',
       'index_right', 'objectid', 'zip', 'shape_area', 'shape_len'],
      dtype='object')
In [27]:
null_zcodes_clean = null_zcodes.drop(columns = ['OBJECTID', 'PROP_ZIP', 'HEIGHT', 'SQMETERS', 'SQFEET', 'LONGITUDE', 'LATITUDE', 'Shape_Length', 'Shape_Area', 'geometry','index_right', 'objectid', 'shape_area', 'shape_len'])
In [28]:
null_zcodes_clean.head()
Out[28]:
BUILD_ID zip
9 34135657 60605
15 34135663 60605
18 34135666 60605
22 34135670 60605
30 34135678 60605

Join the output table of spatial join above, to building structure features (original data) based on unique ID of building structure.¶

In [29]:
cbs_full = pd.merge(cbs, null_zcodes_clean, on = "BUILD_ID", how = "left")
cbs_full
Out[29]:
OBJECTID BUILD_ID PROP_ZIP HEIGHT SQMETERS SQFEET LONGITUDE LATITUDE Shape_Length Shape_Area zip

Populate the null value in the zip code field from the appended (joined) table in those features with null zip code values¶

In [32]:
cbs_full['PROP_ZIP'] = cbs_full['PROP_ZIP'].fillna(cbs_full['zip'])
In [33]:
cbs_full.head(50)

There are still 27 structures with no zip code. However, this is such a small portion of the overall sample that I do not believe it will affect the results. My sample size is n = 557,541.

Clean up data¶

Make a backup copy¶

In [35]:
cbs_full_copy = cbs_full.copy()
In [36]:
cbs_full.columns
Out[36]:
Index(['OBJECTID', 'BUILD_ID', 'PROP_ZIP', 'HEIGHT', 'SQMETERS', 'SQFEET',
       'LONGITUDE', 'LATITUDE', 'Shape_Length', 'Shape_Area', 'zip'],
      dtype='object')
In [37]:
#drop unneccesary columns 
cbs_final = cbs_full.drop(columns = ['SQFEET',
       'LONGITUDE', 'LATITUDE', 'Shape_Length', 'Shape_Area', 'zip'])
In [38]:

Find Mean Building Height By Zip Code¶

In [40]:
cbs_zip_h = cbs_final['HEIGHT'].groupby(cbs_final['PROP_ZIP'])
height_zip = cbs_zip_h.mean()
height_zip = gpd.GeoDataFrame(height_zip)
height_zip
Out[40]:
HEIGHT
PROP_ZIP
60018.0 6.600833
60068.0 5.706000
60601.0 62.295796
60602.0 54.697692
60603.0 54.058889
... ...
60714.0 7.116667
60803.0 4.030000
60804.0 9.997778
60827.0 5.310103
62568.0 6.650000

67 rows × 1 columns

Calculate Building Desity by Zip Code¶

Group Sum of Building Size by Zip Code¶

In [41]:
cbs_zip_sqm = cbs_final['SQMETERS'].groupby(cbs_final['PROP_ZIP'])
sqm_zip = gpd.GeoDataFrame(cbs_zip_sqm.sum())
sqm_zip
Out[41]:
SQMETERS
PROP_ZIP
60018.0 103658.085361
60068.0 791.728386
60601.0 230822.740482
60602.0 151369.638153
60603.0 118729.559261
... ...
60714.0 37315.121582
60803.0 150.924515
60804.0 37674.471268
60827.0 308860.658764
62568.0 96.054054

67 rows × 1 columns

In [42]:
sqm_zip['SQMETERS'].dtype
Out[42]:
dtype('float64')

Find Area of Zip Codes in Square Meters¶

convert to a Projected CRS that is in meters (NAD_1983_NSRS2007_StatePlane_Illinois_East_FIPS_1201) (3528)

In [43]:
zcodes.to_crs(3528, inplace=True)
zcodes.crs
Out[43]:
<Projected CRS: EPSG:3528>
Name: NAD83(NSRS2007) / Illinois East
Axis Info [cartesian]:
- X[east]: Easting (metre)
- Y[north]: Northing (metre)
Area of Use:
- name: United States (USA) - Illinois - counties of Boone; Champaign; Clark; Clay; Coles; Cook; Crawford; Cumberland; De Kalb; De Witt; Douglas; Du Page; Edgar; Edwards; Effingham; Fayette; Ford; Franklin; Gallatin; Grundy; Hamilton; Hardin; Iroquois; Jasper; Jefferson; Johnson; Kane; Kankakee; Kendall; La Salle; Lake; Lawrence; Livingston; Macon; Marion; Massac; McHenry; McLean; Moultrie; Piatt; Pope; Richland; Saline; Shelby; Vermilion; Wabash; Wayne; White; Will; Williamson.
- bounds: (-89.27, 37.06, -87.02, 42.5)
Coordinate Operation:
- name: SPCS83 Illinois East zone (meter)
- method: Transverse Mercator
Datum: NAD83 (National Spatial Reference System 2007)
- Ellipsoid: GRS 1980
- Prime Meridian: Greenwich
In [44]:
zcodes.columns
Out[44]:
Index(['objectid', 'zip', 'shape_area', 'shape_len', 'geometry'], dtype='object')
In [45]:
zcodes['area_sqm'] = zcodes['geometry'].area
In [46]:
zcodes.head()
Out[46]:
objectid zip shape_area shape_len geometry area_sqm
0 1 60607 64664294.2344 39143.6400726 MULTIPOLYGON (((356932.304 579351.246, 356932.... 6.007534e+06
1 2 60661 9357755.8171 13132.5659184 MULTIPOLYGON (((357474.117 580014.401, 357485.... 8.693674e+05
2 3 60606 6766410.78996 12040.4399607 MULTIPOLYGON (((357793.728 580014.703, 357793.... 6.286226e+05
3 4 60615 66565454.6108 38321.3132699 MULTIPOLYGON (((362518.071 570630.23, 362529.5... 6.184158e+06
4 5 60643 207706232.893 75254.7301243 MULTIPOLYGON (((355756.223 561354.369, 355756.... 1.929662e+07
In [47]:
zcodes['area_sqm'].dtype
Out[47]:
dtype('float64')

Find Building Density: Sum of Building Size / Area of Zip Code¶

In [48]:
zcodes = zcodes.rename(columns={'zip': 'PROP_ZIP'})
In [49]:
zcodes['PROP_ZIP'] = zcodes['PROP_ZIP'].astype(float)
In [50]:
zcodes = pd.merge(zcodes, sqm_zip, on = "PROP_ZIP", how = "left")
In [51]:
zcodes['dense_sqm'] = zcodes['SQMETERS']/zcodes['area_sqm']
In [52]:
zcodes.head()
Out[52]:
objectid PROP_ZIP shape_area shape_len geometry area_sqm SQMETERS dense_sqm

Combine False Alert, Building, and Zip Code Data¶

In [53]:
zcodes = pd.merge(zcodes, height_zip, on = "PROP_ZIP", how = "left")
In [54]:
zcodes.head()
Out[54]:
objectid PROP_ZIP shape_area shape_len geometry area_sqm SQMETERS dense_sqm HEIGHT

In [55]:
zcodes = zcodes.rename(columns={'PROP_ZIP': 'ZIP_CODE'})
In [56]:
faz['ZIP_CODE'] = faz['ZIP_CODE'].astype(float)
In [57]:
faz = pd.merge(faz, zcodes, on = "ZIP_CODE", how = "left")
faz.head()

Clean up data¶

In [58]:
faz.columns
Out[58]:
Index(['OBJECTID', 'ZIP_CODE', 'Total_Alerts', 'False_Alerts', 'Shape_Length',
       'Shape_Area', 'objectid', 'shape_area', 'shape_len', 'geometry',
       'area_sqm', 'SQMETERS', 'dense_sqm', 'HEIGHT'],
      dtype='object')
In [59]:
faz = faz.drop(columns=['Shape_Length','Shape_Area','objectid'])
In [60]:
faz = faz.drop([53,54,55])

Create Rate of False Alert¶

In [61]:
faz['Rate_FalseAlerts'] = faz['False_Alerts']/faz['Total_Alerts']

Finished Data Set: False Alert and Structure Data Combined and Organized By Zip Code¶

In [62]:
faz = gpd.GeoDataFrame(faz)
faz.rename(columns={'SQMETERS': 'Sum_BuildSize', 'HEIGHT': 'Mean_BuildHeight'}, inplace=True)
faz

faz_csv = faz.to_csv('data/faz.csv', index = True)

4 5 60607.0 1719.0 1681.0 64664294.2344 39143.6400726 MULTIPOLYGON (((356932.304 579351.246, 356932.... 6.007534e+06 1.561269e+06 0.259885 9.981647 0.977894
In [65]:
faz.columns
Out[65]:
Index(['OBJECTID', 'ZIP_CODE', 'Total_Alerts', 'False_Alerts', 'shape_area',
       'shape_len', 'geometry', 'area_sqm', 'Sum_BuildSize', 'dense_sqm',
       'Mean_BuildHeight', 'Rate_FalseAlerts'],
      dtype='object')

Correlation Analysis¶

False Alerts and Average Building Height¶

In [66]:
#calculate correlation coefficient (Spearman's)
from scipy.stats import spearmanr
rs_h = spearmanr(faz['Mean_BuildHeight'], faz['Rate_FalseAlerts'])
rs_h_corco = rs_h[0]
rs_h_pval = rs_h[1]
rs_h
Out[66]:
SignificanceResult(statistic=np.float64(0.22256269728034664), pvalue=np.float64(0.10919818246045104))
In [67]:
#create scatterplot
fig1 = sns.scatterplot(data=faz, x=faz['Mean_BuildHeight'], y=faz['Rate_FalseAlerts'])
#add correlation coefficient and p-values to plot (Spearman)
plt.text(30,.93, 'Spearman\'s rho = ' + str(round(rs_h_corco,4)), fontsize=10)
plt.text(30,.925, 'Spearman\'s p-value = ' + str(round(rs_h_pval,4)), fontsize=10)
#add labels and title
plt.xlabel("Average Building Height")
plt.ylabel("Rate of False Alerts")
plt.title("Rate of False Alerts and Average Building Height by Zip Code")
Out[67]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Rate of False Alerts and Average Building Height by Zip Code')
In [68]:
height_faz_scatter = fig1.get_figure()
height_faz_scatter.savefig("graphics/faz_height_scatterplot.tiff", dpi=300)
height_faz_scatter.savefig("graphics/png_faz_height_scatterplot.png")

False Alerts and Average Building Density¶

In [69]:
#calculate correlation coefficient (Spearman's)
rs_d = spearmanr(faz['dense_sqm'], faz['Rate_FalseAlerts'])
rs_d_corco = rs_d[0]
rs_d_pval = rs_d[1]
rs_d
Out[69]:
SignificanceResult(statistic=np.float64(0.1416821228701337), pvalue=np.float64(0.3115464710758505))
In [70]:
#create scatterplot
fig2 = sns.scatterplot(data=faz, x=faz['dense_sqm'], y=faz['Rate_FalseAlerts'])
#add correlation coefficient and p-values to plot (Spearman)
plt.text(.07,.92, 'Spearman\'s rho = ' + str(round(rs_d_corco,4)), fontsize=10)
plt.text(.07,.915, 'Spearman\'s p-value = ' + str(round(rs_d_pval,4)), fontsize=10)
#add labels and title
plt.xlabel("Building Density (per SQ Meter)")
plt.ylabel("Rate of False Alerts")
plt.title("Rate of False Alerts and Building Density by Zip Code")
Out[70]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Rate of False Alerts and Building Density by Zip Code')
In [71]:
dense_faz_scatter = fig2.get_figure()
dense_faz_scatter.savefig("graphics/faz_dense_scatterplot.tiff", dpi=300)

Mapping False Alert and Building Structure Variables¶

False Alerts and Average Building Height¶

In [72]:
#Faz Bin List
bins = [.94, .95, .96, .97, .98, .99, 1]
bins_faz = {'bins':bins}
In [73]:
# create height bins list
bins = [6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 15, 24, 48, 61.5]
bins_height = {'bins':bins}
In [74]:
fig3,axes=plt.subplots(nrows=1,ncols=2,figsize=(15,10))
#Map of False Alerts by Zip Code
faz.plot(ax=axes[0],column='Rate_FalseAlerts', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_faz, legend=True, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
axes[0].set_title(f'Rate of False Alerts by Zip Code')
axes[0].axis('off')
#Average Building Height by Zip Code
faz.plot(ax=axes[1],column='Mean_BuildHeight', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_height, legend=True, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
axes[1].set_title(f'Average Building Height by Zip Code (in meters)')
axes[1].axis('off')
Out[74]:
(np.float64(337941.8664412299),
 np.float64(368745.50975123106),
 np.float64(550813.6181958015),
 np.float64(596967.8255258024))
In [75]:
height_faz_map = fig3.get_figure()
height_faz_map.savefig("graphics/faz_height_map.tiff", dpi=300)

False Alerts and Average Building Density¶

In [76]:
fig4,axes=plt.subplots(nrows=1,ncols=2,figsize=(15,10))
#Map of False Alerts by Zip Code
faz.plot(ax=axes[0],column= 'Rate_FalseAlerts', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_faz, legend=True, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
axes[0].set_title(f'Rate of False Alerts by Zip Code')
axes[0].axis('off')
#Map of Average Building Density by Zip Code
faz.plot(ax=axes[1],column= 'dense_sqm', scheme='natural_breaks', k=9, legend=True, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
axes[1].set_title(f'Average Building Density by Zip Code (Square Meters)')
axes[1].axis('off')

C:\Users\jared\miniconda3\envs\geo_env\Lib\site-packages\sklearn\cluster\_kmeans.py:1419: UserWarning: KMeans is known to have a memory leak on Windows with MKL, when there are less chunks than available threads. You can avoid it by setting the environment variable OMP_NUM_THREADS=1.
  warnings.warn(
Out[76]:
(np.float64(337941.8664412299),

Mapping Residuals¶

False Alerts and Average Building Height¶

In [78]:
y = 'Rate_FalseAlerts'
x = 'Mean_BuildHeight'

reg = scipy.stats.linregress(x=faz[x], y=faz[y])
reg
Out[78]:
LinregressResult(slope=np.float64(0.0001337722716848689), intercept=np.float64(0.9672116808336693), rvalue=np.float64(0.08915948873730571), pvalue=np.float64(0.5255070137820768), stderr=np.float64(0.00020925717287986048), intercept_stderr=np.float64(0.003423532186826438))
In [79]:
faz['pred_height'] = reg[0]*faz[x] + reg[1]

faz['resid_height'] = faz[y] - faz['pred_height']

faz[['ZIP_CODE',x,y,'pred_height','resid_height']]

In [80]:
# create a figure with three axes (1 by 3)
fig5,axes=plt.subplots(nrows=1,ncols=3,figsize=(20,15))
# set title for each of three axes
for i,var in enumerate([x,y,'resid_height']):
    if var == 'resid_height':
        faz.plot(ax=axes[i],column=var,scheme='StdMean',cmap='coolwarm',legend=True,legend_kwds={'loc':'lower left'})
        axes[2].set_title(f'Residuals')
        axes[2].axis('off') 
    else:
        faz.plot(ax=axes[0],column='Rate_FalseAlerts', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_faz, legend=True, legend_kwds={'loc':'lower left'}, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
        axes[0].set_title(f'Rate of False Alerts by Zip Code')
        axes[0].axis('off')
        #Average Building Height: User Defined
        faz.plot(ax=axes[1],column='Mean_BuildHeight', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_height, legend=True,legend_kwds={'loc':'lower left'}, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
        axes[1].set_title(f'Average Building Height by Zip Code (in meters)')
        axes[1].axis('off')
In [81]:
height_map_resid = fig5.get_figure()
height_map_resid.savefig("graphics/height_map_resid.tiff", dpi=300)
height_map_resid.savefig("graphics/png_height_map_resid.png")

False Alerts and Average Building Density¶

fig6,axes=plt.subplots(nrows=1,ncols=3,figsize=(20,15))
# set title for each of three axes
for i,var in enumerate([xx,y,'resid_dense']):
    if var == 'resid_dense':
        faz.plot(ax=axes[i],column=var,scheme='StdMean',cmap='coolwarm',legend=True,legend_kwds={'loc':'lower left'})
        axes[2].set_title(f'Residuals')
        axes[2].axis('off') 
    else:
        faz.plot(ax=axes[0],column='Rate_FalseAlerts', scheme = 'UserDefined', classification_kwds=bins_faz, legend=True, legend_kwds={'loc':'lower left'}, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
        axes[0].set_title(f'Rate of False Alerts by Zip Code')
        axes[0].axis('off')
        #Map of Average Building Density by Zip Code
        faz.plot(ax=axes[1],column= 'dense_sqm', scheme='natural_breaks', k=9, legend=True, cmap='YlOrBr', edgecolor='gray')
        axes[1].set_title(f'Average Building Density by Zip Code (Square Meters)')
        axes[1].axis('off')

Code: Explore Other Variables (Tree Canopy Coverage)¶

According to the residuals map, there are some zip codes where Building Height and Building Density underpredicted the Rate of False Alerts. I will investigate whether these zip codes have a high density of trees, as tree coverage is another factor that can lead to GPS errors, therefore creating false alerts in electronic monitoring. My data on tree coverage comes from the 2021 USFS Tree Canopy Cover dataset of the National Land Cover Database (NLCD). I created a layer of the data that only covered Chicago's Zip Codes.

The NLCD dataset is raster data and is not organized by zip code. Since I need to measure tree canopy coverage by zip code, I used the Zonal Statistic tool in Arc GIS to gather the tree coverage raster data by zip code. The "Zone_Stats" table contains the zonal statistics data. Each instance in this table is a Zip Code.

Below is a multiple regression analysis with the Rate of False Alerts as the dependent variable and "Tree Density" and "Average Building Height" as the independent variable. I am defining "Tree Density" as the "SUM" field divided by the "COUNT" field.

Load Zonal Statistics Table¶

In [88]:
zone_stats = pd.read_excel('data/ZonalSt_Chi_Zip1_TableToExcel.xlsx')
In [89]:
zone_stats
Out[89]:

82 rows × 20 columns

Create Tree Density Field¶

In [90]:
tree_dense = zone_stats[['OBJECTID','ZIP_CODE', 'COUNT','SUM']]
tree_dense['tree_dense'] = tree_dense['SUM']/tree_dense['COUNT']
tree_dense

C:\Users\jared\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_13436\42760474.py:2: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  tree_dense['tree_dense'] = tree_dense['SUM']/tree_dense['COUNT']

Start Multiple Regression Analysis : Exploring Data¶

In [94]:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
In [95]:
data = faz_tree
data.columns
Out[95]:
Index(['OBJECTID_x', 'ZIP_CODE', 'Total_Alerts', 'False_Alerts', 'area_sqm',
       'Sum_BuildSize', 'dense_sqm', 'Mean_BuildHeight', 'Rate_FalseAlerts',
       'OBJECTID_y', 'COUNT', 'SUM', 'tree_dense'],
      dtype='object')
In [96]:
# Select features (independent variables) and target (dependent variable)
x = data[['Mean_BuildHeight', 'tree_dense']]  
y = data['Rate_FalseAlerts']
In [97]:
# Split data into training and testing sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
In [98]:
# Create a linear regression model
model = LinearRegression()
In [99]:
# Train the model
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[99]:
LinearRegression()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
LinearRegression()
In [100]:
# Make predictions on the test set
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
In [101]:
# Evaluate the model
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)
print(f'Mean Squared Error: {mse}')
print(f'R-squared: {r2}')

Mean Squared Error: 0.00024293013221539034
R-squared: -0.046597785463304175
In [102]:
# Get coefficients and intercept
print(f'Coefficients: {model.coef_}')
print(f'Intercept: {model.intercept_}')

Coefficients: [0.00026356 0.00118241]
Intercept: 0.9580310059159727
In [103]:
# Visualize predicted vs actual
fig8 = plt.scatter(y_test, y_pred)
plt.xlabel('Actual Values')
plt.ylabel('Predicted Values')
plt.title('Actual vs Predicted')
plt.show()
In [104]:
pred_v_act = fig8.get_figure()
pred_v_act.savefig("graphics/pred_v_act.png")
In [105]:
# Correlation matrix visualization
corr_matrix = data.corr()
fig9 = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()
In [106]:
tree_corr_matrix = fig9.get_figure()
tree_corr_matrix.savefig("graphics/tree_corr_matrix.png")

Additional Sample Code¶

In [107]:
import statsmodels.api as sm
In [108]:
data.columns
Out[108]:
Index(['OBJECTID_x', 'ZIP_CODE', 'Total_Alerts', 'False_Alerts', 'area_sqm',
       'Sum_BuildSize', 'dense_sqm', 'Mean_BuildHeight', 'Rate_FalseAlerts',
       'OBJECTID_y', 'COUNT', 'SUM', 'tree_dense'],
      dtype='object')
In [109]:
# Define the dependent variable and independent variables
y = data['Rate_FalseAlerts']
X = data[['Mean_BuildHeight', 'tree_dense']]
In [110]:
# Add a constant term to the independent variables (for the intercept)
X = sm.add_constant(X)
In [111]:
# Fit the multiple linear regression model
model = sm.OLS(y, X)
results = model.fit()
In [112]:
#Print the regression results, including t-statistics and p-values
print(results.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results

Time:                        13:58:59   Log-Likelihood:                 139.20

Df Residuals:                      50   BIC:                            -266.5

Map Tree Density By Zip Code¶

In [113]:

bins_faz = {'bins':bins}

fig10,axes=plt.subplots(nrows=1,ncols=2,figsize=(15,10))

axes[1].set_title(f'Average Tree Density By Zip Code')
axes[1].axis('off')

Presenting Results¶

Rate of False Alerts and Building Height¶

In [120]:
height_faz_scatter
Out[120]:

The evidence suggests a weak association between the rate of false alerts and building height.

The data on the scatter plot is heavily left-skewed. Of the 53 zip codes, 45 have an average building height of 10 meters or less, which is roughly equivalent to 3 stories or less. The range of false alert rates within these zip codes is the maximum (100%) to the second lowest (92.3077%). Only eight zip codes have an average building height greater than 10 meters.

The average height of these zip codes ranges from 12.97 meters (4 stories) to 62.3 meters (20 stories). Three zip codes have a 100% false alert rate, and their average building heights are 54.7 meters, 5.38 meters, and 4.87 meters. One zip code has the lowest false alert rate (60604), 90.91%, and its average building height is 47.99 meters.

The results of Spearman's correlation analysis between building height and the rate of false alerts yield a coefficient value of 0.2226 with a p-value of 0.1092. This indicates a weak positive curvilinear relationship between building height and a zip code's rate of false alerts. The correlation analysis provided a p-value of .1092. This p-value is not statistically significant, and based on this value, we cannot reject the null hypothesis that there is no association between building height and the rate of false alerts.

In [121]:
fig3
Out[121]:

Figure #3 shows the relationship between the rate of false alerts by zip and the average building height in Chicago. There is a slight spatial pattern: rates of false alerts are high along the shore of Lake Michigan. These areas have a higher average building height, as there are many high-rise buildings along the lakefront. The zip codes with the highest average building height are in the downtown business district (the “Loop”), and zip codes farthest from the Loop have the lowest building height.

Rate of False Alerts and Building Density¶

In [122]:
dense_faz_scatter
Out[122]:

The evidence suggests a weak association between the rate of false alerts and building density.

The zip code with the highest building density (60654) had a false alert rate of 98.26%, the 11th highest false alert rate in the data set.. The zip code with the lowest building density (60633) had a false alert rate of 96.9%.

The analysis provides Spearman’s rho of .1417, indicating a very weak positive curvlinear relationship between building density and the rate of false alerts in a zip code. The correlation analysis also provided a p-value of .3115. This p-value is not statistically significant, suggesting we cannot reject the null hypothesis that there is no association between building density and the rate of false alerts.

In [123]:
fig4
Out[123]:

Figure #4 shows the relationship between the rate of false alerts by zip and the average building height in Chicago. The zip codes with the highest building density are directly adjacent to the central downtown business district. The north and north-west sides of Chicago have higher building density than the south and south-west sides of the city.

There is a slight spatial pattern: the denser zip codes north of the loop also have a higher rate of false alerts, but high false alert rates are not exclusive to those areas.

Mapping Residuals¶

In [124]:
fig5
Out[124]:
In [125]:
fig6
Out[125]:

Figures #5 and #6 show the regression residuals for Rate of False Alerts and Average Building Height and Average Building Density, respectively. The two residual maps are virtually identical, indicating that building height and building density are similar measures of the built environment in terms of false alerts.

In the regression residual maps, red zip codes are where building height/density underpredicted the rate of false alerts, and the blue zip codes are where building height/density overpredicted the rate of false alerts.

The zip codes in dark red are my main areas of interest: there are some zip codes near the loop, but three zip codes are on opposite sides of the city.

My preliminary research indicates these zip codes have a higher tree canopy than the rest of the city, which is why I am exploring tree coverage data as an explanatory variable.

Tree Canopy Coverage and Building Height Residuals¶

In [126]:
fig11
Out[126]:

Fig #11 shows the tree canopy density per zip code in Chicago.

There is a relationship between tree canopy density and the dark red zip codes, where building height/density underpredicted the rate of false alerts. The zip codes at the top left and bottom left of the maps have a higher tree canopy density, and building height and density also underpredicted the rate of false alerts in these areas. However, the dark red zip codes in and near the loop do not have dense tree canopy: it is unlikely that tree coverage is an explanatory variable for the rate of false alerts in these areas.

Further research is needed: Perhaps highway infrastructure is blocking GPS signals in these zip codes? Highway overpasses are not captured in the FEMA structure data. Also, Chicago's Lower Wacker Drive may run through these zip codes. That underground highway is notorious for not having GPS access.

Conclusion¶

Based on the data I received from the Sheriff's Office, the Cook County Electronic Monitoring program has an extraordinarily high rate of false alerts: the minimum rate for false alerts in a zip code was 90.9 percent. Even 60608, the zip code with the highest number of total alerts (n = 24,919), had a false alert rate of 98.37 percent. While there is something amiss with the Electronic Monitoring program, my research suggests that building height and density are unlikely to be the cause of the high rates of false alerts.

My analysis indicates a weak, positive relationship between the rate of false alerts and zip codes with a high average building height and high building density. However, the evidence is not strong enough to reject the null hypothesis that there is no relationship between these variables. Further research is needed to understand the relationship between tree canopy coverage and the rate of false alerts.

Further research is needed to understand why Cook County's Electronic Monitoring system has such a high rate of false alerts. There are real, human consequences for false alerts: people in the justice system have reported harassment from law enforcement officials because of these false alerts. People under electronic monitoring can follow all of the rules and still be treated as a flight risk. Our justice system is committing harm by allowing such a high rate of these false alerts.

In [ ]:
